content.text: What Azure Points of Presence Are

Azure Points of Presence (PoPs) are edge network nodes distributed globally across internet exchange points (IXPs), carrier hotels, and co-location facilities. Unlike Azure regions (which are full compute and storage clusters) or Edge Zones (which are compute extensions), PoPs are network infrastructure: they terminate user connections, cache content, and route traffic onto Microsoft's private WAN backbone as close to the user as possible.

A PoP does not run your application code. It performs three functions: (1) terminates TLS connections so the TLS handshake happens near the user rather than in a distant Azure region, (2) caches static and cacheable content from Azure CDN or Azure Front Door so those requests never travel to the origin, and (3) routes non-cacheable requests to the nearest Azure region over Microsoft's private fiber backbone instead of the public internet.

attrs.alt: Azure PoPs: globally distributed edge nodes that terminate connections, cache content, and route traffic to Azure regions over the Microsoft private backbone

content.text: How PoPs Fit into the Azure Global Network

Microsoft operates one of the largest private WANs in the world — a global fiber network connecting all Azure regions, Edge Zones, and PoPs. This private backbone is distinct from the public internet; traffic on it avoids BGP routing instability, congestion at public peering points, and the unpredictable latency of internet paths.
When a user request arrives at a PoP, the decision tree is:
Is the response cached at the PoP (CDN hit)? → Return the cached response immediately. The request never leaves the PoP.
Is the response cacheable but not yet cached (CDN miss)? → Forward the request to the Azure region origin over the private backbone, cache the response, return it to the user.

Is the request non-cacheable (API call, WebSocket, authenticated request)? → Act as an anycast ingress point, forward the request to the Azure region origin over the private backbone. Return the response to the user. The user benefits from the PoP being the public-internet termination point even though the actual processing is in the region.

The key insight: even for non-cacheable requests, routing through a PoP reduces the time the request spends on the unpredictable public internet. The user-to-PoP hop is a short, low-jitter path; the PoP-to-region hop is on Microsoft's private, QoS-managed fiber.
Azure Services That Use PoPs
PoPs underpin several Azure services. Each service uses the PoP network differently:
Service
What PoPs do for it
Key protocol
Azure CDN (Microsoft tier)
Cache static assets (images, JS, CSS, video segments) at 250+ PoPs globally
HTTP/HTTPS; supports HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 (QUIC)
Azure Front Door
Anycast ingress, TLS termination, routing, WAF enforcement, and optional caching at PoPs
HTTP/HTTPS; WebSocket; gRPC
Azure Traffic Manager
DNS-based routing to the nearest healthy endpoint — PoPs are not in the traffic path directly
DNS (no PoP involvement in data path)
Azure ExpressRoute (Microsoft peering)
Private connectivity from enterprise networks that interconnects at the same PoP/IXP facilities
BGP over private VLAN
Azure Global WAN (Virtual WAN)
Uses PoP routing infrastructure to optimize SD-WAN paths between branch offices and Azure regions
Various (SD-WAN overlay)
Azure CDN PoP Locations and Selection

Azure CDN (Standard Microsoft tier) uses the same PoP infrastructure as Azure Front Door. As of 2024, there are over 250 PoP locations in more than 90 countries. The PoP selection for a given user request is determined by anycast DNS: the user's DNS resolver resolves the CDN endpoint hostname to the IP address of the nearest PoP, as measured by BGP path length and round-trip time.
You cannot manually select which PoP serves a request. The anycast routing layer handles this automatically. You can, however, influence behavior through:
Cache rules:
configure TTL, query-string caching behavior, and cache key normalization in the CDN profile to maximize the cache-hit rate at PoPs.
Origin groups:
define origin groups with health probes so that on a cache miss the PoP forwards to the healthiest and closest origin.
Geo-filtering:
block or allow requests from specific countries at the PoP level — the request is rejected before it reaches the origin.
Rules Engine:
route requests to different origins based on URL path, headers, or device type at the PoP level.

# Create an Azure CDN profile (Microsoft tier uses Front Door PoP network)
az cdn profile create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCDNProfile \
  --sku Standard_Microsoft \
  --location global

# Create a CDN endpoint pointing to an Azure Storage origin
az cdn endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --name myEndpoint \
  --origin myaccount.blob.core.windows.net \
  --origin-host-header myaccount.blob.core.windows.net \
  --enable-compression true \
  --content-types-to-compress text/plain text/html application/json text/css application/javascript

# Set a custom caching rule: cache /assets/* for 7 days
az cdn endpoint rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --name myEndpoint \
  --order 1 \
  --rule-name "AssetsCache" \
  --match-variable UrlPath \
  --operator BeginsWith \
  --match-values "/assets/" \
  --action-name CacheExpiration \
  --cache-behavior Override \
  --cache-duration "7.00:00:00"

# Check CDN endpoint cache behavior from a specific PoP region
az cdn endpoint show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myCDNProfile \
  --name myEndpoint \
  --query "{optimizationType:optimizationType, origins:origins[0].hostName}" \
  --output table
Azure Front Door and PoPs

Azure Front Door is the primary product built on top of the PoP network. It combines CDN caching, TLS termination, load balancing, WAF, and HTTP routing into a single globally distributed service. Every rule configured in Front Door executes at the PoP nearest to the user — not in the Azure region — which gives Front Door its low-latency characteristics for dynamic content.
The Front Door architecture is: user → nearest PoP (TLS, WAF, cache check, routing decision) → private backbone → origin (Azure region or on-premises).

# Create an Azure Front Door Standard profile
az afd profile create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --sku Standard_AzureFrontDoor

# Add an origin group pointing to an App Service
az afd origin-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --origin-group-name myOriginGroup \
  --probe-request-type GET \
  --probe-protocol Https \
  --probe-interval-in-seconds 30 \
  --probe-path "/health" \
  --sample-size 4 \
  --successful-samples-required 3

az afd origin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --origin-group-name myOriginGroup \
  --origin-name myAppService \
  --host-name myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --origin-host-header myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --http-port 80 \
  --https-port 443 \
  --priority 1 \
  --weight 1000

# Add an endpoint (the user-facing hostname)
az afd endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --endpoint-name myFDEndpoint \
  --enabled-state Enabled

# Create a route to forward requests to the origin group
az afd route create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --endpoint-name myFDEndpoint \
  --route-name defaultRoute \
  --origin-group myOriginGroup \
  --supported-protocols Https \
  --https-redirect Enabled \
  --forwarding-protocol HttpsOnly \
  --patterns-to-match "/*"
PoPs vs Regions vs Edge Zones: The Three Tiers
Understanding where PoPs sit relative to regions and Edge Zones prevents architectural confusion. The three tiers serve different functions:
Tier
What runs there
Does it run your code?
Distance to user
Primary function
Azure Region
Full compute, storage, PaaS, management plane
Yes — VMs, containers, PaaS services
10–500ms (region-dependent)
Stateful workloads, databases, AI, general compute
Azure Edge Zone
Compute subset (VM, AKS, limited PaaS)
Yes — VM, AKS, containers
1–5ms (carrier co-lo in city)
Ultra-low-latency stateful compute (5G, gaming, industrial IoT)
Azure PoP
Network infrastructure: TLS termination, CDN cache, routing
No — network functions only
<10ms (IXP or carrier hotel in or near city)
Content caching, TLS handshake, traffic acceleration to region

A well-designed globally distributed application uses all three tiers: PoPs for content delivery and connection termination, Edge Zones for ultra-low-latency compute where needed, and Azure Regions for stateful application logic, databases, and PaaS services.
Monitoring and Diagnosing CDN and Front Door Traffic at PoPs

Azure Monitor and Azure Diagnostics expose metrics and logs from the PoP layer for both CDN and Front Door. These logs identify which PoP served a request, the cache status (HIT or MISS), request latency, and any WAF actions taken at the PoP.

# Enable diagnostic logging for a Front Door profile to Log Analytics
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Cdn/profiles/myFrontDoor \
  --name fdDiagnostics \
  --workspace /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myLAW \
  --logs '[{"category":"FrontDoorAccessLog","enabled":true},{"category":"FrontDoorHealthProbeLog","enabled":true},{"category":"FrontDoorWebApplicationFirewallLog","enabled":true}]' \
  --metrics '[{"category":"AllMetrics","enabled":true}]'

# Query: which PoPs are serving the most traffic (Log Analytics KQL)
# Run in Log Analytics workspace:
# AzureDiagnostics
# | where ResourceType == "FRONTDOORS"
# | where Category == "FrontDoorAccessLog"
# | summarize count() by pop_s, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
# | order by count_ desc

# Query: cache hit rate per PoP
# AzureDiagnostics
# | where Category == "FrontDoorAccessLog"
# | summarize
#     hits   = countif(cacheStatus_s == "HIT"),
#     misses = countif(cacheStatus_s == "MISS"),
#     total  = count()
#   by pop_s
# | extend hitRate = round(100.0 * hits / total, 1)
# | order by hitRate asc

# Purge cached content from all PoPs (force cache invalidation after a deployment)
az afd endpoint purge \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --endpoint-name myFDEndpoint \
  --content-paths "/*" \
  --domains myFDEndpoint.z01.azurefd.net

Cache purges propagate to all PoPs globally within a few minutes. Until the purge completes, some PoPs may still serve stale content. For time-sensitive deployments (security patches, urgent content updates), use versioned URLs (e.g., /assets/app-v2.js) rather than relying on purge propagation timing.
PoP Health and Failover

Azure automatically monitors PoP health and reroutes traffic when a PoP becomes unhealthy. If a PoP has a connectivity issue, anycast routing shifts traffic to the next-nearest PoP without requiring any action from the application owner. This failover is transparent — the user's DNS resolution may return a different PoP IP, but the endpoint hostname stays the same.

Azure SLA for Front Door Standard/Premium is 99.99% availability. This SLA is backed by the automatic PoP failover mechanism, multi-PoP health probing of origins, and the redundancy built into Microsoft's backbone between PoPs and regions.

# Test which PoP you are connecting to (using curl verbose output)
# The 'x-azure-ref' header returned by Front Door contains the PoP identifier
curl -s -I https://myFDEndpoint.z01.azurefd.net/ | grep -i "x-azure-ref\|x-cache\|x-ms-ref"

# Check origin health probe results in Front Door
az afd origin show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --origin-group-name myOriginGroup \
  --origin-name myAppService \
  --query "{enabledState:enabledState, weight:weight, priority:priority}"

# List all active Front Door endpoints (verify global reachability)
az afd endpoint list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --query "[].{name:name, hostName:hostName, enabledState:enabledState}" \
  --output table
Cache Strategy: Maximizing PoP Hit Rate

The primary performance lever for CDN and Front Door is the cache hit rate — the fraction of requests served from the PoP cache rather than forwarded to the origin. A 95% hit rate means 95% of requests never reach your origin servers, dramatically reducing origin load and reducing latency for those requests to single-digit milliseconds.

The cache key is the string used to look up content in the PoP cache. By default the cache key is the full URL including query string. Cache key configuration mistakes are the most common cause of low hit rates:
Query string variation:

if your URL includes tracking parameters like ?utm_campaign=email&utm_source=newsletter, every unique UTM combination is a separate cache entry — the same content, millions of cache misses. Strip or ignore irrelevant query parameters using the Rules Engine query string normalization.
Missing Vary normalization:

if your origin sends Vary: Accept-Encoding headers and some clients send gzip while others send br (Brotli), the PoP may treat them as separate cache entries. Configure Front Door compression and let Front Door normalize encoding negotiation.
Short TTLs:

if the origin sets Cache-Control: max-age=60, content expires at the PoP every minute. For static assets that change only on deployment, use Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 (one year) with versioned URLs, not short TTLs.
Authentication headers in cache key:

never cache authenticated API responses unless the cache key includes the user identity. The default behavior is to not cache responses when Authorization headers are present; do not override this for private content.

# Configure Front Door Rules Engine to strip UTM parameters from cache key
az afd rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --rule-set-name cacheRules \
  --rule-name stripUTM \
  --order 1 \
  --match-variable RequestUri \
  --operator Contains \
  --match-values "utm_" \
  --action-name RouteConfigurationOverride \
  --query-string-caching-behavior IgnoreSpecifiedQueryStrings \
  --query-parameters "utm_source" "utm_medium" "utm_campaign" "utm_content" "utm_term"

# Check current cache hit rate in Azure Monitor
# (Run in Log Analytics workspace after enabling FrontDoorAccessLog)
# AzureDiagnostics
# | where Category == "FrontDoorAccessLog" and TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
# | summarize
#     total = count(),
#     hits  = countif(cacheStatus_s in ("HIT", "REMOTE_HIT"))
#   by bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
# | extend hitPct = round(100.0 * hits / total, 1)
# | project TimeGenerated, hitPct, hits, total
# | order by TimeGenerated desc

# View Front Door metrics in Azure Monitor
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Cdn/profiles/myFrontDoor \
  --metric "RequestCount" "CacheHitRatio" \
  --interval PT5M \
  --aggregation Total Average \
  --start-time 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-01-01T01:00:00Z \
  --output table
WAF at the PoP Layer

Azure Front Door Premium supports the Azure Web Application Firewall (WAF) at the PoP layer. This means WAF rule evaluation (OWASP ruleset, custom rules, bot protection) executes at the PoP closest to the attacker, not at the origin. Malicious requests are dropped at the edge without consuming origin compute, bandwidth, or database connections.
WAF in Front Door operates in two modes:
Detection mode:
all requests pass through to the origin; WAF logs the requests that would have been blocked. Use Detection mode when first deploting to identify false positives before enforcing blocks.
Prevention mode:
requests matching WAF rules are blocked at the PoP with a 403 response. Switch to Prevention mode after validating that legitimate traffic is not matched by the rules.

# Create a WAF policy for Front Door
az network front-door waf-policy create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWAFPolicy \
  --sku Premium_AzureFrontDoor \
  --mode Detection

# Associate managed ruleset (Microsoft Default Ruleset)
az network front-door waf-policy managed-rules add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --type Microsoft_DefaultRuleSet \
  --version 2.1

# Add bot protection ruleset
az network front-door waf-policy managed-rules add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --type Microsoft_BotManagerRuleSet \
  --version 1.0

# Attach WAF policy to Front Door security policy
az afd security-policy create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myFrontDoor \
  --security-policy-name mySecPolicy \
  --waf-policy /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/frontdoorwebapplicationfirewallpolicies/myWAFPolicy \
  --domains /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Cdn/profiles/myFrontDoor/afdEndpoints/myFDEndpoint

# Switch WAF to Prevention mode after validating
az network front-door waf-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWAFPolicy \
  --mode Prevention

WAF false positives are the primary risk when switching to Prevention mode. Always run Detection mode for at least one week of production traffic before switching. Review WafLog entries in Log Analytics for rule matches against legitimate user agents, API clients, and crawlers before enabling blocking.
ExpressRoute and PoP Interconnection

Microsoft's ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering uses the same PoP/IXP facilities as the CDN and Front Door PoPs. When you enable ExpressRoute Microsoft Peering, you receive BGP routes for Microsoft's public IP prefixes (including Office 365 and Azure public services) through the ExpressRoute circuit. The traffic path for Microsoft Peering is: your on-premises network → ExpressRoute circuit → Microsoft PoP → Microsoft private backbone → destination service.

This means your Office 365 or Azure CDN traffic can reach Microsoft's network at the nearest PoP without traversing the public internet, reducing latency and improving reliability for enterprise branch-to-cloud traffic.
ExpressRoute Peering Type
Route advertisements
Traffic destination
Relevant to PoPs?
Private Peering
Your Azure VNet address space
Azure VMs, private IPs in your VNets
No — bypasses PoPs entirely
Microsoft Peering
Microsoft public IP prefixes (Azure, M365)
Azure public services, CDN, Front Door, Office 365
Yes — traffic enters Microsoft network at PoP
Common PoP Architecture Patterns
Two patterns appear in most production deployments that use PoPs effectively:
Pattern 1: Static Assets via CDN, Dynamic API via Front Door

Separate static and dynamic traffic. Static assets (images, fonts, JS/CSS bundles, video segments) go through Azure CDN for maximum cache effectiveness. Dynamic API calls and HTML pages go through Front Door for WAF protection, health-check-based routing, and origin failover. Both use the same underlying PoP network.

Static asset URLs (e.g., assets.example.com) point to a CDN endpoint backed by Azure Blob Storage. Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 is set on blobs; versioned filenames eliminate cache invalidation problems.

API and HTML URLs (e.g., www.example.com) point to a Front Door endpoint. WAF runs in Prevention mode. Origin group has two origins (primary region and secondary region) with health probes on /health.
Deployment pipeline: upload new static assets to Blob Storage (new versioned filenames), then deploy new app code to the App Service origins. No cache purge required because filenames changed.
Pattern 2: Global Acceleration for Private APIs

For internal APIs that must be accessed from global branch offices or remote workers, Front Door with Private Link origin provides PoP-accelerated access to an origin that has no public IP. Traffic from any branch goes to the nearest PoP, traverses Microsoft's backbone, and exits through a Private Link connection to the origin's private VNet — never touching the public internet from PoP to origin.

# Create a Front Door Premium profile (required for Private Link origins)
az afd profile create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myPremiumFD \
  --sku Premium_AzureFrontDoor

# Add an origin with Private Link to an App Service (no public access needed on App Service)
az afd origin create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --profile-name myPremiumFD \
  --origin-group-name myPrivateGroup \
  --origin-name myPrivateOrigin \
  --host-name myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --origin-host-header myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --https-port 443 \
  --priority 1 \
  --weight 1000 \
  --private-link-resource /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/myapp \
  --private-link-location eastus \
  --private-link-sub-resource-type sites

# After approval of Private Endpoint in App Service portal, verify connection
az network private-endpoint-connection list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myapp \
  --type Microsoft.Web/sites \
  --query "[].{name:name, status:properties.privateLinkServiceConnectionState.status}" \
  --output table
Summary

Azure Points of Presence are network edge nodes distributed at over 250 locations globally, built on Microsoft's private WAN backbone. They provide TLS termination, CDN caching, and traffic routing acceleration for Azure CDN, Azure Front Door, and other network services.
PoPs are network infrastructure — they do not run application code. For compute at the edge, use Azure Edge Zones or Azure Regions.
The user-to-PoP hop is short and low-jitter; the PoP-to-region hop uses Microsoft's private fiber backbone, bypassing public internet routing instability.
Azure CDN and Azure Front Door are the primary services that expose PoP capabilities to application developers.
Cache hit rates determine how much of your traffic stays at the PoP versus traveling to the origin region. Optimize TTL and cache keys for maximum hit rate.
PoP failover is automatic and transparent — anycast DNS routes around unhealthy PoPs without requiring application changes.
Use diagnostic logs (FrontDoorAccessLog) to identify per-PoP traffic distribution, cache hit rates, and WAF actions for performance tuning.
